label: delphi-persistent
system:
You are the neutral facilitator of a Delphi panel on a legal question.
You NEVER evaluate arguments, take positions, or hint at a correct answer. You only
organize what panelists said, anonymously. Any editorializing is a failure.
prompt:
Final-round anonymized Delphi answers:

```json
[
  {
    "probability": 71,
    "position_summary": "Modest upward revision from my round-1 67 to 71. I remain convinced the core structural argument is sound: under Thomson v. Call, a disqualifying \u00a7 1090 interest held by a sitting body member is not cured by that member's own recusal, so if Okafor's community-property stake in her spouse's Harbor Light salary counts as a \"financial interest ... in the contract,\" the award is void/voidable regardless of how carefully she recuses. What moved me is the panel's near-unanimous view (Themes E/F) that no enumerated \u00a7 1091(b) remote-interest or \u00a7 1091.5 noninterest category cleanly covers a spouse's salaried, non-officer employment with the contracting nonprofit \u2014 meaning the \"fixed salary, non-contingent on this particular contract\" defense I leaned on in round 1 likely has no statutory home that would make it curable by disclosure-and-abstention even if accepted, and instead mostly bears on whether a \"financial interest\" exists at all (a threshold question, not a curability one). That reframing pushes outcomes toward \"interest exists and is uncurable by recusal\" more often than toward \"interest is remote and curable.\" I did not move as far as the panel median because I still credit a genuine, unresolved doctrinal gap: some authority and practical reasoning support treating highly attenuated, general-operating-budget-funded, non-contract-contingent employment interests as falling short of the \"financial interest\" threshold entirely (closer to a PRA-materiality question), which would mean \u00a7 1090 \u2014 and its no-cure-by-recusal rule \u2014 never engages in the first place. The proposition's binary framing (void or voidable) doesn't fully accommodate that possibility, so I keep meaningful weight on it.",
    "rationales": [
      "Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633's structural rule \u2014 that \u00a7 1090's prohibition runs to the contracting body as a whole, so an interested member's recusal does not itself validate a contract the body makes \u2014 is essentially undisputed across the panel (7/7) and remains the load-bearing premise for the proposition; I give it high confidence.",
      "Family Code \u00a7 760 community-property principles give Okafor a personal (not merely derivative) pecuniary stake in half her spouse's ~$95K salary, and Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v.       "The panel's convergence that no enumerated \u00a7 1091(b) remote-interest category or \u00a7 1091.5 noninterest provision squarely covers a spouse's salaried (non-officer, non-fixed-fee) employment with the contracting nonprofit is a genuinely new-to-me consideration: it means even a persuasive 'fixed, non-contingent salary' argument may lack a statutory mechanism to become curable-by-disclosure, and instead just feeds into a prior, separate question of whether \u00a7 1090 is triggered at all \u2014 this is what actually moved my number up.",
      "I continue to withhold full deference to the panel median because a real doctrinal fork remains open and, in my view, under-resolved even in the summary: whether an interest this attenuated (general operating budget, only ~40% city-funded, spouse's compensation not contingent on any single award, Harbor Light's continued operation and her continued employment not plausibly turning on this one RFP) clears the 'financial interest ... in the contract' threshold under \u00a7 1090 at all, versus being properly analyzed only under PRA \u00a7\u00a7 87100/87103 materiality (where recusal is the established, sufficient cure) \u2014 this is a threshold classification question logically prior to, and distinct from, the remote-interest curability question the panel focused on.",
      "Okafor's own uncompensated board seat plausibly falls within a \u00a7 1091.5-type noninterest for noncompensated nonprofit liaisons/officers (consistent with 6/7 panelists), which would remove one prong of the problem but does not reach the spousal-salary interest, which remains the dispositive and more serious defect driving my probability."

    "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether the spousal-employment interest even clears \u00a7 1090's \"financial interest ... in the contract\" threshold in the first place, given its attenuation (general-operating-budget funding, only ~40% government-derived, salary not contingent on any single award) \u2014 versus assuming that threshold is met and asking only whether an exception applies. The panel largely analyzed this as an exception-fit question, but if a court instead resolves it as a threshold non-interest question (more akin to PRA materiality), \u00a7 1090's no-cure-by-recusal rule may never engage, and the contract could survive on a well-executed recusal record alone. I cannot confidently resolve which framing a reviewing court would adopt on these facts.",
    "response_to_panel": "The consideration that actually moved me was Theme F/Disagreement 2: the panel's fairly strong showing (4/7 explicitly, more implicitly) that the enumerated \u00a7 1091(b)/\u00a7 1091.5 catalog has no category that squarely fits a spouse's salaried, non-officer employment with a contracting nonprofit. In round 1 I treated 'fixed, non-contract-contingent salary' as a live remote-interest escape hatch under something like \u00a7 1091(b); the panel discussion made me realize that even if that factual characterization is correct, it may not map onto any actual statutory remote-interest category, so it wouldn't make the interest curable-by-disclosure \u2014 it would just bear on whether the interest exists at all. That reallocation of where the doubt sits pushed my number up modestly. I did not fully adopt the panel median because Disagreement 3 (threshold 'is this even a \u00a7 1090 interest, or a PRA-materiality-only question') persuades me there remains a genuine, separate escape route the panel didn't fully resolve either, and I'm not willing to treat majority head-count on a contested doctrinal classification as itself evidence of the correct legal answer \u2014 Delphi convergence here reflects shared professional priors more than a resolved authority, so I moved only partway rather than to the median/mean."

      "The residual, unresolved disagreement among panelists over whether some unspecified \u00a7 1091(b) category could reach spousal employment, plus the lack of a squarely on-point civil (non-criminal) appellate holding on this exact fact pattern (designated liaison board seat plus spouse's fixed nonprofit salary), justifies a modest one-point reduction from my round-1 figure but not a larger move toward the panel median."

    "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a reviewing court would find that a spouse's fixed, non-contingent, general-operating-budget salary crosses the threshold from a generalized economic stake in the employer's continued solvency into a disqualifying 'interest in the contract' itself under \u00a7 1090 -- and relatedly, whether some narrowly-drawn \u00a7 1091(b) remote-interest category (which I cannot fully verify from memory) might be argued to reach spousal employment with a contracting nonprofit, an issue on which the panel itself is genuinely split and several cited authorities remain unverified.",

I considered most seriously the round-1 disagreement about where the threshold doubt sits (is this a \u00a7 1090 'interest' question at all, or really a PRA materiality question curable by recusal?), since that seemed like a potentially outcome-determinative reframing I hadn't isolated explicitly before. On reflection I concluded it cuts in favor of my existing position, not against it: spousal employment with a contracting party is treated in CA municipal-conflict practice as a cognizable \u00a7 1090-type interest in its own right, independent of PRA analysis, so this doesn't lower my number. I gave real weight to the unresolved split (disagreement point 2) over whether any \u00a7 1091(b) remote-interest category could reach spousal employment, since I cannot fully verify the current statutory catalog from memory and the panel itself was split with unverified citations on both sides -- that is the one point that genuinely warranted trimming my estimate slightly, from 78 to 77, rather than holding flat or reverting toward the 72 median."

The proposition holds board membership and spousal employment constant and varies only recusal \u2014 this is close to the paradigm Thomson v. Call / Stigall fact pattern: a governing-body member's disqualifying financial interest (whether located in the community-property-imputed spousal salary, the board seat itself, or both) is not cured by that member's abstention, because \u00a7 1090's prohibition runs to the body's composition, not just the vote. The panel's unanimous convergence on that structural rule (Theme A, 7/7) and on the spousal salary as the central candidate interest (Theme B, 7/7), combined with no panelist identifying a concretely-sourced \u00a7 1091/1091.5 provision that clearly reaches a director-level spouse's substantial, contract-dependent salary, keeps me at the high end of the panel range rather than pulling me toward the median.",

Code \u00a7\u00a7 751/760), plus 6/7 endorsing the revenue-dependence nexus (40% contract funding, 60% historical win rate) as pushing the interest toward direct/material, reinforces rather than undermines my prior reasoning \u2014 no panelist surfaced authority showing courts treat a fixed, general-operating-budget salary as categorically outside \u00a7 1090's 'financial interest' reach.",
      "Disagreement point 2 (whether any \u00a7 1091(b) remote-interest category reaches spousal employment with the contracting nonprofit) remains genuinely unresolved across the panel \u2014 several panelists asserted no such category applies (Theme F, 4/7 affirmative), none identified verified statutory text establishing one does; unresolved ambiguity in a strict-construction, prophylactic statute (Stigall, Honig) should if anything be resolved toward disqualification, not away from it.",

The unanimous 7/7 agreement on the two load-bearing pillars of my original position \u2014 that recusal cannot cure a body member's \u00a7 1090 interest (Theme A) and that the spousal salary is the central, community-property-imputed interest (Theme B) \u2014 read to me as corroboration, not grounds for reversion to consensus, since no panelist offered a countervailing authority or statutory text that would undercut either pillar. The genuine disagreement over \u00a7 1091(b)'s scope for spousal employment (Disagreement 2) is real but cuts, if anything, toward disqualification under strict-construction doctrine (Stigall/Honig) when the exception catalog's fit is unproven rather than affirmatively established. I treated Disagreement 4 (whether resignation could cure) as inapplicable here since the proposition holds both the board seat and spousal employment fixed and tests recusal alone. I did not move down to the median merely because most other panelists landed lower; I found no specific theme or disagreement point in the summary that supplied a doctrinal reason to discount my analysis, only reasons that were already incorporated into my round-1 uncertainty band."

Statistics: Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 75.0, mean 75.0, std dev 2.3, range 71.0–79.0, middle 50% of panel between 74.0 and 77.0.

The panel has finished. Characterize what disagreement PERSISTS after mutual feedback: (a) the crux each side rests on, (b) whether the split traces to a genuinely unsettled legal question, a factual assumption, or differing risk tolerance, and (c) what a court or new fact would have to resolve. Persistent disagreement is signal — report it precisely; do not adjudicate it.
text:
# Persistent Disagreement Report — Final Round

**Overall posture:** The panel converged directionally (all seven ≥ 71; median/mean 75.0; std dev 2.3). No panelist disputes the proposition's direction. The residual 8-point spread is driven not by disagreement over the conclusion but by disagreement over *where the surviving doubt lives* and *how much it is worth*. Three distinct fault lines persist.

---

## Fault Line 1: The threshold-classification question (§ 1090 "financial interest" vs. PRA-only)

**(a) Crux each side rests on**

- **Lower cluster (71, and the two 74s):** A spouse's fixed, non-contingent salary paid from a general operating budget only ~40% city-funded may fail to clear the § 1090 "financial interest … in the contract" threshold *at all*. If so, the matter is governed only by PRA §§ 87100/87103 materiality analysis — where recusal is the recognized and sufficient cure — and § 1090's no-cure-by-recusal rule never engages. The panelist at 71 explicitly notes the binary void/voidable framing does not accommodate this outcome, and one 74 states this threshold question is "where the real probability mass of 'proposition false' lives."
- **Upper cluster (77, 79):** The threshold question resolves *in favor of* finding an interest. The 77 panelist reports that spousal employment with a contracting party is treated in California municipal-conflict practice (ILG/AG guidance, Honig-adjacent reasoning) as a cognizable § 1090 interest independent of PRA analysis; the 79 panelist notes no panelist surfaced authority treating a fixed general-operating-budget salary as categorically outside § 1090.

**(b) Nature of the split:** A **genuinely unsettled legal question** — panelists on both sides characterize it as an unresolved doctrinal fork, and no one produced a verified, squarely on-point civil authority classifying this fact pattern. It is compounded by a **factual-characterization dispute over the same undisputed facts**: the 40% budget dependence / 60% win rate / director-level seniority are read by some (both 74s, 75s, 77, 79) as converting the interest into "direct and material," and by the 71 panelist as consistent with attenuation below the threshold.

**(c) What would resolve it:** A California appellate decision or AG opinion directly classifying a spouse's fixed, non-contingent salary at a nonprofit contractor as (or as not) a § 1090 interest; or new facts tying the spouse's position or compensation more directly to this specific contract (e.g., the position is contract-funded, or would be eliminated if the award were lost), which multiple panelists indicated would collapse the attenuation argument.

---

## Fault Line 2: The § 1091(b) exception-catalog question

**(a) Crux each side rests on**

- One 74 panelist keeps live the possibility that a § 1091(b) remote-interest category addressing employees of a contracting party could be **extended via imputation to spousal employment**, making the defect curable by disclosure-and-abstention without resignation.
- The 75s, 77, and 79 treat the catalog as containing no fitting category; the 79 panelist adds that under strict-construction doctrine (Stigall/Honig), unresolved ambiguity about exception fit should be resolved *toward* disqualification — a canon-allocation argument no other panelist adopted or rebutted.
- The 71 panelist accepted the "no fitting category" premise but redirected its significance: absence of an exception pushes the doubt back to Fault Line 1 rather than eliminating it.

**(b) Nature of the split:** Partly a **genuinely unsettled legal question** (scope of the enumerated categories as applied to spouses), but substantially an **evidentiary/verification gap**: multiple panelists (both 74s, 75, 77) expressly state they cannot verify the current statutory text of § 1091(b) and § 1091.5(a), or the actual holding and citation of *Fraser-Yamor Agency v. County of Del Norte*, from memory. One panelist flagged that "a meaningful part of my upward revision loses its footing" if Fraser-Yamor does not hold what the panel recalled.

**(c) What would resolve it:** Verification of the current statutory text of §§ 1091(b) and 1091.5(a); confirmation of Fraser-Yamor's actual holding; or an authority addressing whether any remote-interest category reaches spousal (as opposed to the official's own) employment with a contracting party.

---

## Fault Line 3: How to structure and price the residual uncertainty

**(a) Crux each side rests on**

- One 75 panelist *reduced* their estimate specifically because they now see two **conjunctive** uncertain steps (interest-exists AND no-exception-applies) rather than one dominant uncertainty.
- The 79 panelist held near the top of the range on the ground that this same doubt was already priced into their round-1 band and that convergence supplied corroboration, not correction.
- The 71 panelist declined to move toward the median on the stated ground that Delphi convergence "reflects shared professional priors more than a resolved authority" and that majority head-count on a contested doctrinal classification is not itself evidence.

**(b) Nature of the split:** This fault line is **not doctrinal** — it reflects **differing epistemic/risk postures**: how to aggregate stacked uncertainties, and how much evidential weight (if any) to give panel convergence itself. Panelists reached different numbers from substantially overlapping doctrinal views.

**(c) What would resolve it:** Nothing external; this is a calibration-philosophy difference. It would dissolve only if Fault Lines 1 and 2 were resolved by authority, at which point the conjunction collapses.

---

## Points of no persistent disagreement (for completeness)

- The Thomson v. Call / Stigall structural rule — recusal does not cure a sitting member's disqualifying interest — was affirmed by all seven and is treated by every panelist as the load-bearing premise.
- Community-property imputation (Fam. Code §§ 751/760) as the mechanism making the spousal salary Okafor's *own* interest was accepted panel-wide.
- The separateness of the board-seat and spousal-salary interests — and that a § 1091.5-type cure of the former would not reach the latter — drew no final-round dissent.
- One panelist noted Disagreement 4 from earlier rounds (resignation as cure) is moot under the proposition's framing, which holds board seat and employment constant; no panelist contested that.

**Bottom line:** The persistent disagreement is narrow but precise: whether an attenuated spousal-salary interest is analyzed as a *threshold* § 1090 question (potentially exiting the statute entirely into PRA territory) or as an *exception-fit* question within § 1090 — a genuinely unsettled classification that no panelist could resolve with verified authority — overlaid with an unverified-sources problem and divergent calibration philosophies. The panel's spread maps almost exactly onto how much weight each panelist assigns the threshold-classification escape route.
